text: The surgeon navigates around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to harvest lymph nodes, carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon encounters bleeding from a lymph node while attempting to skeletonize the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon identifies pockets of lymph nodes embedded within the adipose tissue, aiming to excise these nodes along with the surrounding fat.
The surgeon attempts circumferential dissection around the artery, focusing on carefully separating surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to perform precise dissection around the artery, aiming to achieve circumferential exposure and harvest lymph nodes.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect above and below the artery, aiming to elevate the surrounding lymph nodes with the specimen.
The surgeon transects the tissue, resulting in visible bleeding at the site of transection.
The surgeon attempts to control bleeding by using a vessel sealer to grasp the tissue, rather than using electrocautery with the Maryland device.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Prolene suture to perform a figure-eight stitch, ensuring the suture is locked to prevent the short tail from being pulled out.
The surgeon prepares to staple the tissue, navigating through a significant amount of adipose tissue. The stapling process proceeds smoothly.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the surgical site, observing the area for any bubbling, which indicates that everything is intact and functioning properly.
The surgeon instructs the assistant to approach the staple line for documentation. Bubbling is observed next to a silk suture, indicating a potential leak.
The surgeon applies a second layer of reinforcement on the staple line, visible over the patient's adipose tissue.
The surgeon uses an Alexis wound protector to retract the edges of the incision while closing the peritoneum. After completing this step, the surgeon undocks and closes the fan and seal incision.
